stem: A vertical telephone pole is 36 feet tall. A technician anchors the pole with two support cables, each making a 53-degree angle with the ground. The first cable attaches at the top of the pole, and the second cable attaches at a point 12 feet above the ground. The pole also carries a 200-pound transformer near the top. Using the approximations sin(53) = 4/5 and cos(53) = 3/5, what is the total length of both support cables, in feet?
explanation: The transformer weight (200 pounds) is extra information that should be ignored. For each cable, sin(53) = height/cable length. Cable 1 (top, height 36): sin(53) = 36/c1, so c1 = 36/(4/5) = 45 feet. Cable 2 (12 feet up): sin(53) = 12/c2, so c2 = 12/(4/5) = 15 feet. Total length = 45 + 15 = 60 feet.

distractorRationale.B: Used cos(53) instead of sin(53) to find cable lengths: c1 = 36/(3/5) = 60, c2 = 12/(3/5) = 20, total = 60 + 20 = 80
distractorRationale.C: Only computed the cable from the top of the pole and forgot to add the second cable: c1 = 36/(4/5) = 45
distractorRationale.D: Computed the horizontal ground distances from the base of the pole instead of the cable lengths: ground1 = 36/tan(53) = 36/(4/3) = 27, ground2 = 12/tan(53) = 12/(4/3) = 9, total = 27 + 9 = 36

imageDescription: A vertical line represents the telephone pole, labeled 36 ft tall. Two diagonal lines represent support cables running from points on the pole to the ground on the right. The upper cable attaches at the top of the pole (36 ft), and the lower cable attaches at a point marked 12 ft above the ground. Both cables make 53-degree angles with the horizontal ground line. The ground distances from the pole base to each cable's anchor point are not labeled.
